title: Result
text:
This section presents the results of the comparative textual analysis of the privacy policies of Flo, Clue, and Ovia. The results are organized in two stages. First, each app is analyzed separately using the same coding categories developed in the methods section. Second, the three cases are compared to identify broader patterns in how femtech apps communicate data collection, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, and sensitive reproductive health data practices.
The purpose of this section is not only to summarize what each privacy policy says, but also to evaluate whether the disclosures are specific and understandable enough to support meaningful informed consent. All three apps provide formal privacy disclosures, but the clarity, scope, and usability of those disclosures vary significantly.

title: Result
section: Flo,Clue,Ovia
text:
Flo,Clue,Ovia

Flo
Flo’s privacy policy is highly detailed and provides substantial information about the types of personal and reproductive health data it collects. It explicitly discusses menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms, sex life, physical and mental health, and data imported from third-party health platforms such as Apple HealthKit and Google Health Connect. Flo also provides detailed explanations of advertising and analytics partners, including AppsFlyer, Firebase, and TikTok Ad Manager.
However, Flo’s policy is also complex. Its explanations of third-party data processing, advertising, analytics, legal bases, and consent settings require users to understand multiple data flows across several external platforms. This creates a tension between formal disclosure and practical transparency: Flo discloses a lot, but the amount and complexity of the disclosure may still make it difficult for ordinary users to fully understand how their data is used.







Coding Category
Flo




What sensitive data is collected?
Flo collects general account information such as name, email, birth month and year, password, location, time zone, and language. More importantly, it collects reproductive and well-being data, including weight, height, BMI, body temperature, menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms linked to menstrual cycle, pregnancy, perimenopause, or menopause, sex life, physical health, mental health, water intake, and sleep duration.


How is data collection explained?
Flo explains that personal data can come directly from the user, automatically from device and app usage, from third-party services such as Apple HealthKit and Google Health Connect, and from external sources. This structure helps separate user-provided data from automatically collected data and imported data.


Is sensitive reproductive data clearly labeled?
Partly yes. Flo explicitly refers to “special category data” and names menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms, sex life, and physical and mental health. This makes the sensitive nature of the data relatively visible. However, these categories are still embedded within a long policy that may be difficult for users to process.


Is third-party sharing mentioned?
Yes. Flo states that it does not share personal data with third parties except as described in the policy. It discusses sharing with service providers, advertising and analytics platforms, commercial partners, telehealth partners, research institutions, and other processors.


Is advertising mentioned?
Yes. Flo gives detailed explanations of advertising and promotional data processing. It discusses AppsFlyer, Firebase, TikTok Ad Manager, Google services, ad networks, and Apple AppTrackingTransparency. Flo states that it does not share health data with third parties for marketing or advertising purposes, but it may share technical identifiers, subscription status, age group, app launch activity, and advertising identifiers for promotional purposes.


Is analytics mentioned?
Yes. Flo discusses analytics in relation to app performance, promotional campaigns, advertising optimization, usage trends, Firebase, AppsFlyer, TikTok Ad Manager, and Google services. It also says usage activity may be reviewed to make sure the app works correctly, fix issues, serve relevant content, and improve user experience.


What vague phrases appear?
Flo uses several broad or flexible phrases, including “may collect,” “may share,” “legitimate interests,” “improve our Services,” “enhance your experience,” “partners,” “business acquisition, transfer or reorganisation,” “as required by applicable law,” “in some cases,” and “other similar unique technical identifiers.”


Does it have a plain-language summary?
Partly. Flo uses headings, examples, tables, and visual diagrams to explain some third-party data flows. This is helpful, especially for advertising and analytics. However, the policy remains long and complex, and users must move across multiple sections to understand the full picture.


Data retention
Flo states that it keeps personal data for as long as necessary to provide services or fulfill the purposes for which it was collected. If a user deactivates the account or requests deletion, Flo says it processes the request within one month, although full deletion from backup systems may take up to 90 days. If the app is deleted or the account becomes inactive, Flo retains personal data for three years before deletion unless the user requests earlier deletion.


Security measures
Flo states that it uses encryption in transit and at rest, vulnerability scanning, data integrity protection, organizational and legal measures, and privacy assessments. However, it also states that it cannot guarantee absolute security.




Preliminary Finding: Flo
Flo provides one of the most detailed privacy disclosures among the three apps. It is relatively explicit about the types of sensitive reproductive health data it collects, and it gives detailed descriptions of advertising and analytics partners. This level of detail is important because it gives users more information than a generic privacy policy would.
At the same time, detail does not automatically equal clarity. Flo’s policy requires users to understand distinctions between health data, device data, advertising identifiers, service providers, processors, commercial partners, and telehealth partners. Even when Flo states that it does not share health data with advertising partners, users must still interpret how other data types, such as age group, subscription status, app launch activity, IP address, and advertising identifiers, may move through advertising and analytics systems. Therefore, Flo demonstrates the central problem of this project: formal disclosure exists, but meaningful informed consent may still be limited by complexity.



Clue
Clue’s privacy policy is the most clearly organized of the three policies analyzed. It separates the data it processes into three main categories: account data, usage data, and health data. Clue defines health data as sensitive information that users choose to track in the app, including cycle information, period length, pain, spotting, weight, body temperature, hair quality, and sexual intercourse. It also explains that health data may be entered manually or imported from integrations such as Apple Health or supported fitness trackers. :contentReferenceoaicite:0
Compared with Flo and Ovia, Clue’s privacy policy is more explicit about separating health data from other types of data. It also repeatedly states that sensitive health data is not shared with advertisers. However, the policy is still complex because Clue relies on many third-party providers for hosting, communication, monitoring, customer support, advertising optimization, website analytics, surveys, and research.







Coding Category
Clue




What sensitive data is collected?
Clue collects health data and other sensitive data that users choose to track in the app. This includes cycle information such as period length, pain, spotting, and depending on what the user provides, weight, body temperature, hair quality, and sexual intercourse. It also collects account data such as username, date of birth, and email address. :contentReferenceoaicite:1


How is data collection explained?
Clue explains data collection through three main categories: account data, usage data, and health data. Usage data includes device data, browser data, IP address, approximate location, crash information, and app interaction data. Health data can be entered manually or imported from integrations such as Apple Health or supported fitness trackers. :contentReferenceoaicite:2


Is sensitive reproductive data clearly labeled?
Yes. Clue directly labels reproductive and cycle-tracking information as “Health data.” This category explicitly includes cycle information and other sensitive tracked experiences. This makes the reproductive health data category relatively clear compared with policies that rely mostly on broad terms such as “personal information” or “sensitive information.”


Is third-party sharing mentioned?
Yes. Clue names multiple third-party providers, including AWS, Braze, Datadog, Firebase Cloud Messaging, SparkPost, Slack, Zendesk, Growthdot, research partners, Apple Search Ads, Google Ads, Adjust, Google Analytics, and Snowplow. Some providers, such as Braze, Datadog, Firebase Cloud Messaging, and SparkPost, are described as essential and cannot be opted out of. :contentReferenceoaicite:3


Is advertising mentioned?
Yes. Clue says it processes usage data and technical data, such as device ID and IP address, to optimize advertising. It uses Apple Search Ads, Google Ads, and Adjust. Through Adjust, Clue shares standard app events such as app installation or Clue Plus subscription with ad networks such as Meta and TikTok. However, Clue states that Adjust does not have access to tracked personal health data. :contentReferenceoaicite:4


Is analytics mentioned?
Yes. Clue discusses health analytics, technical app improvement, website analytics, advertising analytics, and research analytics. For health analytics, Clue states that processing is managed internally and that no third-party providers are used for that purpose. For website analytics, Clue uses cookies and third-party tracking services such as Google Analytics. :contentReferenceoaicite:5 :contentReferenceoaicite:6


What vague phrases appear?
Clue uses some broad phrases, including “carefully selected research partners,” “improve our services,” “personalized recommendations,” “relevant products,” “certain third-party providers,” “legitimate interest,” “as soon as it is no longer required,” and “material to your consent.” These phrases are less vague than some terms in the other policies, but they still require interpretation.


Does it have a plain-language summary?
Partly yes. Clue uses a relatively readable structure and separates data types clearly. It also provides short consent statements that users may toggle on or off in privacy settings. However, the full policy is still long and includes many third-party providers, legal bases, and data processing purposes.


Data retention
Clue repeatedly states that personal data collected for each purpose is deleted as soon as it is no longer required for the purpose for which it was collected. It also states that users can request complete deletion of their data, including past data sent to third-party services used for tracking and analysis, and that deletion will occur within one month. :contentReferenceoaicite:7


Security measures
Clue states that account/profile data is stored separately from health data, giving health data a higher level of protection. It also stores data on EU servers, uses password hashing and salting, HTTPS encrypted transmission, and does not store payment information. :contentReferenceoaicite:8




Preliminary Finding: Clue
Clue’s policy is comparatively strong in terms of structure and clarity. By separating account data, usage data, and health data, Clue makes it easier for users to understand that reproductive health information is different from ordinary account or device information. Clue also repeatedly emphasizes that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers, which directly addresses one of the major ethical concerns surrounding femtech apps.
However, Clue’s policy still illustrates the limits of privacy disclosure as a consent mechanism. Even though the structure is clearer than Flo’s and Ovia’s, the policy still describes many third-party providers and different processing purposes. Some tools are essential and cannot be opted out of. Other forms of processing depend on toggles or consent settings. As a result, users still need to understand a fairly complex data ecosystem in order to make an informed choice.



Ovia
Ovia’s privacy policy has the broadest scope of the three policies. This is because Ovia is not only a fertility and pregnancy app, but also provides parenting features, health coaching, employer or health-plan sponsored Ovia+ services, research-related uses, and deidentified or aggregated data services. Ovia’s policy explicitly lists fertility, pregnancy, health, sex life, life circumstances, child health and milestone information, legally protected classifications, biometric information, geolocation, inferences, and sensitive information. :contentReferenceoaicite:9
Ovia’s policy is especially important for this project because it shows how privacy disclosure becomes more complicated when reproductive health apps are connected to employers, health plans, health care providers, research partners, and commercial data uses. The policy states that Ovia no longer shows advertising to consumer users and does not sell or share personal data for advertising as of June 2025, but it also includes a historical table describing categories of data that were previously sold or shared for advertising. :contentReferenceoaicite:10







Coding Category
Ovia




What sensitive data is collected?
Ovia collects fertility, pregnancy, health, sex life, and life circumstances information that users input into the apps or discuss through health coaching. In the Parenting app, it may collect health and milestone information about a child. It also lists sensitive information such as racial or ethnic origin, health information, sex life, and sexual orientation information. :contentReferenceoaicite:11


How is data collection explained?
Ovia organizes collected data into categories such as identifiers, general personal information including health information, legally protected classifications, biometric information, internet and app activity, geolocation, audio and visual information, professional and employment-related information, inferences, and sensitive information. This categorical structure is useful, but the scope is very broad. :contentReferenceoaicite:12


Is sensitive reproductive data clearly labeled?
Partly. Ovia explicitly names fertility, pregnancy, health, sex life, pregnancy status, due date, and date of last period. However, these data points are often placed inside broad categories such as “general personal information including health information” and “sensitive information,” rather than being separated into a narrower reproductive health data category.


Is third-party sharing mentioned?
Yes. Ovia mentions employers, health plans, health care providers, AWS, U.S.-based vendors, research partners such as universities and medical research institutions, other businesses that engage Ovia for research services, advertisers and sponsors for past promotions, and advertising or analytics providers before Ovia ended its advertising business. :contentReferenceoaicite:13 :contentReferenceoaicite:14


Is advertising mentioned?
Yes. Ovia states that as of June 2025 it no longer shows advertising to consumer users and does not sell or share personal data for advertising. However, the policy also includes a historical table showing that before ending its advertising business, Ovia sold or shared certain categories of personal data for advertising, including online identifiers, IP addresses, app/ad engagement information, and in some opt-in promotions, pregnancy due month/year. :contentReferenceoaicite:15 :contentReferenceoaicite:16


Is analytics mentioned?
Yes. Ovia says it uses personal data to understand how users use the Services, including what content and ads they view; obtain analytics about app performance; market, develop, and improve Ovia’s and its affiliates’ products and services; create deidentified and aggregated data; and conduct clinical and scientific research. :contentReferenceoaicite:17


What vague phrases appear?
Ovia uses broad phrases such as “Comprehensive Use,” “legitimate interests,” “lawful conduct of its business,” “uses not permitted by law,” “other businesses,” “research and development purposes,” “as permitted by law,” “proper business records,” and “where required by law.” The phrase “Comprehensive Use” is especially important because it applies many purposes across multiple data categories.


Does it have a plain-language summary?
Partly. Ovia has a shorter “Notice of Collection” and uses tables to organize categories of data. However, the policy is still complicated because it distinguishes between Consumer and Ovia+ users, includes employer and health-plan relationships, discusses deidentified data and aggregated data, and includes a historical advertising-sharing table.


Data retention
For Consumer users, Ovia stores all categories of data while the account is active and for 7 years afterward, then automatically deletes it. For Ovia+ users, Ovia stores data while the user receives Ovia+ benefits and generally deletes it after benefits end. Users can also delete their data at any time, subject to exceptions. :contentReferenceoaicite:18


Security / storage
Ovia states that data is transferred to servers in the United States through AWS and may be transferred to other U.S.-based vendors. It also warns that non-U.S. residents’ data may become subject to U.S. jurisdiction, which may provide weaker data protection safeguards and rights than the user’s home jurisdiction. :contentReferenceoaicite:19


Deidentified / aggregated data
Ovia may use personal data to create deidentified data for research and development. It may provide deidentified data to research partners such as universities and medical research institutions and to other businesses that engage Ovia for research services. Ovia may also disclose or sell deidentified data derived from patient information and may receive compensation for sharing deidentified or aggregate data. :contentReferenceoaicite:20




Preliminary Finding: Ovia
Ovia’s policy raises the most complex informed consent issues because its data ecosystem is broader than a standard menstrual tracking app. Ovia collects and processes data not only for app functionality, but also for fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, employer or health-plan benefits, research, deidentified data, aggregated data, and product development. This makes the privacy policy harder to interpret because users must understand whether they are using the Consumer version or Ovia+, whether their data is connected to an employer or health plan, and how deidentified or aggregated data may be shared or monetized.
Ovia’s statement that it no longer sells or shares personal data for advertising is important. However, the policy’s historical advertising table shows that advertising-related sharing existed before June 2025. This matters because it demonstrates how privacy practices can change over time and how users may need to understand not only current data practices but also past or transitional practices. Ovia’s policy therefore shows how privacy disclosure can become especially burdensome when reproductive health data is connected to multiple institutional and commercial contexts.

title: Result
section: Cross-Case Comparison
text:
Cross-Case Comparison
The three privacy policies show that formal disclosure is present across all cases. Flo, Clue, and Ovia each provide a public privacy policy, identify categories of collected data, discuss third-party processing, mention advertising or analytics, and describe retention or deletion practices. However, the policies differ substantially in how clearly they communicate these practices.







App
Main Pattern




Flo
Provides detailed explanations of sensitive data collection and advertising/analytics partners, but the policy is long and complex.


Clue
Provides the clearest structure by separating account data, usage data, and health data, and repeatedly states that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers.


Ovia
Has the broadest data scope because it includes fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, Ovia+, employer/health-plan contexts, research, and deidentified or aggregated data.

title: Result
section: Result
text:
Result

Result 1: All Three Apps Disclose Sensitive Reproductive Health Data Collection
All three apps disclose that they collect sensitive reproductive or health-related data. Flo explicitly lists menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms, sex life, and physical and mental health information. Clue identifies health data as a separate category and includes cycle information, period length, pain, spotting, weight, body temperature, hair quality, and sexual intercourse. Ovia includes fertility, pregnancy, health, sex life, life circumstances, child health and milestone information, and legally protected classifications.
The main difference is not whether the apps disclose sensitive data collection, but how they organize and explain it. Clue provides the clearest categorization by separating account data, usage data, and health data. Flo provides detailed examples, but the information is spread across a long and complex policy. Ovia lists many categories, but its broad structure makes it harder to isolate reproductive health data from other sensitive or institutional data uses.


Result 2: Third-Party Sharing Is Disclosed, but Data Flows Are Complex
Each policy mentions third-party providers, but the complexity of third-party relationships differs. Flo discusses advertising and analytics partners in detail, including AppsFlyer, Firebase, TikTok Ad Manager, Google services, commercial partners, telehealth partners, and processors. Clue identifies a wide range of third-party providers, including AWS, Braze, Datadog, Firebase Cloud Messaging, SparkPost, Slack, Zendesk, Adjust, Google Analytics, and research partners. Ovia discusses AWS, vendors, employers, health plans, health care providers, research partners, other businesses, and historical advertising partners.
These disclosures are important because they show that femtech data practices are not limited to the app company alone. Data may move through hosting providers, analytics tools, communication platforms, advertising attribution systems, research partners, and institutional partners. Even when companies state that health data is not shared with advertisers, users must still understand how other data types, such as device identifiers, IP addresses, usage events, subscription status, or inferred information, may be shared.


Result 3: Advertising and Analytics Are Treated Differently Across the Three Apps
Advertising and analytics are among the most important areas for informed consent. Flo provides the most detailed explanation of advertising and analytics partners and states that health data is not shared for advertising. Clue also states that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers and separates health analytics from advertising analytics. Ovia states that it no longer shows advertising to Consumer users and no longer sells or shares personal data for advertising as of June 2025, but its policy includes a historical table explaining previous advertising-related data sharing.
These differences show that advertising disclosure is not simply a yes-or-no issue. A policy may say that health data is not shared with advertisers, while still allowing technical identifiers, IP addresses, app events, or advertising identifiers to be processed for campaign optimization. This distinction is important, but it may not be obvious to ordinary users. Users may not clearly understand the difference between “health data,” “usage data,” “technical data,” “device identifiers,” and “advertising identifiers.”


Result 4: Broad and Ambiguous Language Appears in All Three Policies
All three policies contain broad language that gives companies flexibility. Flo uses phrases such as “may share,” “legitimate interests,” “improve our Services,” and “partners.” Clue uses phrases such as “carefully selected research partners,” “relevant products,” “certain third-party providers,” and “as soon as it is no longer required.” Ovia uses especially broad terms such as “Comprehensive Use,” “legitimate interests,” “lawful conduct of its business,” “other businesses,” and “as permitted by law.”
These phrases do not necessarily mean that the companies are hiding information. In many cases, they may reflect legal requirements or the need to describe complex technical systems. However, from the perspective of meaningful informed consent, broad language creates a problem: it shifts interpretive burden onto users. Users must decide what these phrases mean, how broad the company’s discretion is, and whether their sensitive reproductive health data could be used in ways they did not expect.


Result 5: Formal Disclosure Does Not Guarantee Practical Transparency
The strongest overall finding is that the existence of a privacy policy does not guarantee meaningful informed consent. All three apps disclose important information, but the disclosures are long, technical, legalistic, and distributed across many sections. Clue is the clearest of the three, but even Clue requires users to understand multiple legal bases, third-party providers, consent toggles, and analytics categories. Flo provides detailed explanations but may overwhelm users with complexity. Ovia provides broad categorical disclosures, but its Consumer/Ovia+ distinction, institutional partnerships, deidentified data practices, and historical advertising table make the policy difficult to interpret.
This finding supports the central argument of the project: privacy policies should not be treated as equivalent to meaningful informed consent. In the context of reproductive health data, meaningful consent requires more than formal notice. It requires disclosures that are specific, readable, and usable for ordinary users.

title: Result
section: Summary
text:
Summary
Overall, the analysis shows that Flo, Clue, and Ovia all provide formal privacy disclosures, but they vary in clarity and usability. Clue offers the clearest data categorization and the strongest separation between health data and advertising data. Flo provides extensive detail, especially about advertising and analytics, but its policy may be difficult for ordinary users to navigate. Ovia has the broadest and most complex data environment because it includes pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, employer or health-plan services, research, deidentified data, aggregated data, and historical advertising practices.

This results applies the course concept of operationalization by turning the abstract idea of meaningful informed consent into observable textual indicators, including clarity around sensitive data collection, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, and vague policy language.
The result first makes descriptive claims about what Flo, Clue, and Ovia disclose in their privacy policies. It then makes normative recommendations about how femtech privacy disclosures should be redesigned to better support meaningful informed consent.
From a deontological perspective, meaningful consent is necessary to respect user autonomy. From a consequentialist perspective, unclear privacy disclosures can lead users to underestimate the risks of sharing sensitive reproductive health data.

The results suggest that the central privacy issue in femtech apps is not simply whether companies disclose data practices. The deeper issue is whether users can realistically understand those disclosures. This distinction is especially important because menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, sexual activity, and reproductive health data are highly sensitive. When privacy policies rely on long explanations, broad categories, and complex third-party relationships, user consent may be formally obtained but not meaningfully informed.

title: Introduction
text:
Femtech applications have become an important part of digital health. Apps such as Flo, Clue, and Ovia allow users to track menstrual cycles, fertility windows, pregnancy, symptoms, sexual activity, physical health, mental health, and other reproductive health information. These apps can provide useful services, such as cycle predictions, ovulation estimates, pregnancy support, personalized health insights, and reminders. For many users, they may also create a sense of control over their bodies and health decisions.
However, the data collected by these apps is not ordinary app data. Menstrual cycle information, fertility goals, pregnancy status, symptoms, sexual activity, and reproductive health notes are highly sensitive. These data points can reveal intimate details about a person’s body, health, sexuality, family planning, and life circumstances. Because of this sensitivity, femtech apps raise important questions about privacy, consent, and data governance.
In digital platforms, privacy policies are often used as the main mechanism for informing users about data practices. Companies publish privacy policies to explain what data they collect, how they use it, whether they share it with third parties, how long they keep it, and what choices users have. In theory, these policies support informed consent. Users are expected to read the policy, understand the company’s data practices, and decide whether to use the service.
In practice, privacy policies often do not function this way. They can be long, complex, technical, and legalistic. They may also use broad or ambiguous phrases such as “may share,” “legitimate interests,” “partners,” “service providers,” “business purposes,” “improve our services,” or “as permitted by law.” These phrases may be useful for companies because they provide flexibility, but they may not give users a clear understanding of how their data actually moves through advertising, analytics, research, service provider, or institutional systems.
This problem is especially serious for reproductive health apps. If a user does not clearly understand how their menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, or sexual health data is collected, shared, analyzed, or retained, then their consent may be formal rather than meaningful. A user may click “I agree” or continue using an app without fully understanding how sensitive reproductive health data or related technical data may be used. Therefore, the existence of a privacy policy should not automatically be treated as proof of meaningful informed consent.
This project examines the privacy policies of three femtech applications: Flo, Clue, and Ovia. These apps were selected because they are widely known reproductive health platforms and because they represent different kinds of femtech data environments. Flo focuses on menstrual cycle, ovulation, fertility, pregnancy, and health tracking. Clue focuses on period and reproductive health tracking and clearly separates account data, usage data, and health data in its policy. Ovia has a broader scope because it includes fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, Ovia+, employer or health-plan benefits, research, and deidentified or aggregated data.
The main research question guiding this project is:
How do menstrual tracking and women’s health apps use privacy policy language to describe the collection, sharing, and use of sensitive reproductive health data, and what does this reveal about the limits of meaningful informed consent?
This project argues that privacy policies should not be evaluated only by whether they exist. Instead, they should be evaluated based on whether they are clear, specific, readable, and usable for ordinary users. In other words, meaningful informed consent requires more than formal disclosure. It requires that users can realistically understand what sensitive data is collected, why it is collected, whether it is shared with third parties, whether it is used for advertising or analytics, how long it is retained, and what choices users have.
To answer the research question, this project uses the publicly available privacy policies of Flo, Clue, and Ovia as textual data. The analysis applies comparative textual analysis and codes each policy for key categories, including sensitive data collection, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, data retention, security, plain-language explanation, and vague or broad wording. This method allows the project to compare how each app communicates its data practices and whether those communications support meaningful informed consent.
The project also uses the course concept of operationalization. The conceptual variable in this project is meaningful informed consent. A weak operationalization of this concept would be the mere existence of a privacy policy or the fact that a user clicked “I agree.” This project uses a stronger operationalization: whether the policy clearly explains sensitive reproductive health data practices in a way that ordinary users can understand. This distinction is important because a privacy policy can satisfy formal notice requirements while still failing to provide practical transparency.
The findings show that Flo, Clue, and Ovia all disclose important information about data collection and use, but they differ in clarity and complexity. Flo provides detailed explanations, especially around advertising and analytics, but the policy is long and complex. Clue has the clearest structure because it separates account data, usage data, and health data, and repeatedly states that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers. Ovia has the broadest data environment because it includes fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, employer or health-plan services, research, and deidentified or aggregated data.
Overall, this project shows that the central privacy issue in femtech apps is not simply whether companies disclose data practices. The deeper issue is whether users can realistically understand those disclosures. Because reproductive health data is highly sensitive and context-dependent, privacy policy ambiguity should be treated as a data ethics and governance problem, not merely a communication problem.

title: Policy Recommendations
text:
The findings of this project show that Flo, Clue, and Ovia all provide formal privacy disclosures, but formal disclosure alone is not enough to guarantee meaningful informed consent. Each app explains some aspects of data collection, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, and user choices. However, the policies are still long, complex, and difficult for ordinary users to fully understand. This is especially concerning because femtech apps collect highly sensitive reproductive health data, including menstrual cycle information, fertility data, pregnancy information, symptoms, sexual activity, physical and mental health information, and in some cases child-related or employer-related data.
The policy recommendations below are based on the central finding of this project: privacy policies should not be treated as equivalent to meaningful informed consent. Meaningful consent requires disclosures that are clear, specific, readable, and usable. In the context of femtech apps, regulators and companies should move beyond long legal privacy policies and create stronger standards for reproductive health data transparency.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Standardized Plain-Language Privacy Summaries
text:
Require Standardized Plain-Language Privacy Summaries
The first recommendation is that regulators should require femtech apps to provide a standardized plain-language privacy summary at the beginning of the privacy policy and inside the app before users agree to data processing.
The analysis shows that all three apps disclose important information, but the information is often spread across many sections. Flo provides detailed explanations of advertising and analytics partners, but the policy is complex. Clue is better organized, especially because it separates account data, usage data, and health data, but it is still long and contains many third-party providers and legal bases. Ovia provides a table and a shorter notice, but its policy is complicated because it covers Consumer users, Ovia+ users, employers, health plans, research partners, deidentified data, and aggregated data.
A plain-language summary would reduce the burden on users. Instead of requiring users to search through long legal documents, the app should provide a short standardized disclosure that answers the most important questions immediately.
A required femtech privacy summary should include:







Required Summary Item
Explanation




What sensitive data is collected?
The app should clearly list menstrual data, fertility data, pregnancy data, symptoms, sexual activity, health conditions, mental health information, and any child-related data.


Why is the data collected?
The app should explain whether the data is used for core app functions, predictions, personalization, research, advertising, analytics, employer benefits, or product development.


Is the data shared with third parties?
The app should identify categories of third parties, such as analytics providers, advertising platforms, cloud providers, research partners, employers, health plans, or health care providers.


Is the data used for advertising?
The app should clearly state whether reproductive health data, usage data, technical identifiers, or inferences are used for advertising, ad attribution, or campaign optimization.


Is the data used for analytics or research?
The app should explain whether data is used for health analytics, app analytics, scientific research, product development, deidentified datasets, or aggregated statistics.


How long is the data retained?
The app should state a specific retention period or explain when data is deleted.


Can users delete or export their data?
The app should explain how users can delete, correct, download, or opt out of certain processing.



The summary should be written at an accessible reading level. It should avoid legalistic phrases such as “as permitted by law,” “legitimate interests,” “business purposes,” or “Comprehensive Use” unless those terms are immediately explained in ordinary language.
This recommendation does not require companies to stop collecting data or offering personalized services. Instead, it requires them to communicate data practices in a way that ordinary users can understand. This directly addresses the problem identified in the literature review: users cannot meaningfully consent if the privacy information is too long, complex, or difficult to interpret.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Create Mandatory Sensitive Reproductive Data Labels
text:
Create Mandatory Sensitive Reproductive Data Labels
The second recommendation is that femtech apps should be required to label sensitive reproductive health data as a distinct category. Companies should not be allowed to hide menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, sexual activity, or reproductive health information inside broad categories such as “personal information,” “health information,” “general personal information,” or “sensitive information” without further explanation.
This recommendation is based on the comparison of Flo, Clue, and Ovia. Clue provides a relatively strong example because it separates data into account data, usage data, and health data. This makes it easier for users to see that reproductive health data is different from ordinary account or device data. Flo also explicitly identifies menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms, sex life, and physical and mental health information as sensitive or special category data. Ovia names many sensitive data types, including fertility, pregnancy, health, sex life, sexual orientation, and pregnancy status, but these are often placed inside broad categories such as “general personal information including health information” and “sensitive information.”
A stronger policy standard would require femtech apps to use a clear label such as:

Sensitive Reproductive Health Data

Under this label, the app should list every relevant type of data collected. For example:








Sensitive Reproductive Health Data Type
Collected?
Example




Menstrual cycle data
Yes/No
Period dates, cycle length, spotting, pain


Fertility data
Yes/No
Ovulation estimates, fertile window, trying-to-conceive status


Pregnancy data
Yes/No
Due date, pregnancy week, pregnancy symptoms


Sexual activity data
Yes/No
Sexual intercourse, contraception, sex life information


Symptom data
Yes/No
Cramps, mood, pain, discharge, nausea


Health condition data
Yes/No
Medical conditions, mental health, physical health


Child or parenting data
Yes/No
Baby milestones, child health information


Inferred reproductive data
Yes/No
Pregnancy trimester, fertile window, predicted period



This label would help users recognize that reproductive health information deserves special attention. It would also prevent companies from making sensitive data less visible by placing it under broad legal categories. The goal is not only to disclose data collection, but to make the sensitivity of the data obvious.
This recommendation also connects to contextual integrity. Users may understand that a period tracking app needs period dates to provide predictions. However, they may not expect reproductive data or inferred reproductive status to be used for advertising, analytics, employer benefits, health plan services, research, or product development. A sensitive data label would help users understand the context in which their data is being collected and the contexts into which it may move.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require a Standardized Femtech Data Use Table
text:
Require a Standardized Femtech Data Use Table
The third recommendation is that regulators should require femtech apps to include a standardized data use table. This table should show, for each type of data, whether it is collected, why it is used, whether it is shared, whether it is used for advertising, whether it is used for analytics or research, and how long it is retained.
This recommendation comes directly from the findings of this project. One of the biggest problems across Flo, Clue, and Ovia is that users must move across many sections of the privacy policy to understand the full data flow. For example, a user may need to read one section about data collection, another about advertising, another about analytics, another about research, another about third-party providers, another about retention, and another about user rights. Even when the company discloses the information, the structure makes it difficult to compare and understand.
A standardized table would make privacy information more usable. The table could look like this:












Data Type
Collected?
Purpose
Shared with Third Parties?
Used for Ads?
Used for Analytics or Research?
Retention Period




Account data
Yes
Login, account management, communication
Service providers
No/Yes
Yes/No
Specific period


Menstrual cycle data
Yes
Cycle prediction, health insights
Yes/No
No/Yes
Yes/No
Specific period


Pregnancy data
Yes
Pregnancy tracking, personalization
Yes/No
No/Yes
Yes/No
Specific period


Sexual activity data
Yes/No
Health tracking, personalization
Yes/No
No/Yes
Yes/No
Specific period


Device identifiers
Yes
App function, analytics, advertising attribution
Yes/No
Yes/No
Yes/No
Specific period


IP address
Yes
Security, analytics, approximate location
Yes/No
Yes/No
Yes/No
Specific period


Inferences
Yes/No
Personalization, predictions, recommendations
Yes/No
Yes/No
Yes/No
Specific period


Deidentified data
Yes/No
Research, product development, statistics
Yes/No
No/Yes
Yes/No
Specific period



This type of table would make it easier for users to compare data practices across apps. It would also help researchers, journalists, and regulators evaluate whether companies are being clear about sensitive reproductive data.
The table should be mandatory and standardized across femtech apps. If every company uses a different format, users still face a comparison problem. Standardization would reduce confusion and make privacy disclosures more transparent.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Specific Disclosure of Third-Party Sharing
text:
Require Specific Disclosure of Third-Party Sharing
The fourth recommendation is that femtech apps should be required to disclose third-party sharing in a more specific way. It is not enough to say that data may be shared with “service providers,” “partners,” “vendors,” or “third parties.” These terms are too broad for users to understand the actual data flow.
The findings show that all three apps rely on third-party providers. Flo discusses advertising and analytics partners such as AppsFlyer, Firebase, TikTok Ad Manager, and Google services. Clue names providers such as AWS, Braze, Datadog, Firebase Cloud Messaging, SparkPost, Slack, Zendesk, Adjust, Google Analytics, and Snowplow. Ovia mentions AWS, U.S.-based vendors, employers, health plans, health care providers, research partners, and other businesses that may receive deidentified or aggregated data.
Because third-party ecosystems are complex, users need a clearer disclosure standard. Apps should be required to disclose:

the name or category of the third party;
the type of data shared;
the purpose of sharing;
whether sensitive reproductive health data is included;
whether the third party can use the data for its own purposes;
whether the data leaves the user’s country or legal jurisdiction;
whether users can opt out.

A standardized third-party sharing table could look like this:











Third Party Category
Example Provider
Data Shared
Purpose
Includes Reproductive Health Data?
Opt-Out Available?




Cloud hosting provider
AWS
Account data, app data
Storage and infrastructure
Yes/No
No


Analytics provider
Google Analytics / Firebase / Adjust
Usage data, device identifiers, IP address
App or website analytics
Yes/No
Yes/No


Advertising partner
TikTok, Meta, Google Ads
Advertising identifiers, app events
Campaign optimization
Yes/No
Yes/No


Customer support provider
Zendesk / Slack
Account data, support content
User support
Yes/No
Yes/No


Research partner
University or medical institution
Deidentified health data
Scientific research
Yes/No
Yes/No


Employer or health plan
Employer/health plan partner
Eligibility or benefit data
Ovia+ services
Yes/No
Yes/No



This recommendation is important because third-party sharing is one of the hardest parts of privacy policies for users to understand. A user may not know the difference between a processor, controller, vendor, service provider, advertising network, or research partner. A specific table would make these relationships more visible.
This recommendation would also help reduce vague language. Instead of saying “we may share data with partners,” companies would need to explain what kind of partner, what kind of data, and for what purpose.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Separate Advertising, Analytics, and Core Service Processing
text:
Separate Advertising, Analytics, and Core Service Processing
The fifth recommendation is that femtech apps should be required to clearly separate three different types of data processing:

core service processing;
analytics and product improvement;
advertising and marketing.

These categories should not be blended together. Users should be able to understand which data is necessary for the app to function and which data is used for secondary purposes.
This recommendation is important because the analysis shows that advertising and analytics are disclosed differently across the three apps. Flo provides detailed explanations of advertising and analytics partners and states that health data is not shared for advertising. Clue states that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers and separates health analytics from advertising analytics. Ovia states that it no longer shows advertising to Consumer users and does not sell or share personal data for advertising as of June 2025, but its policy also includes a historical table explaining prior advertising-related sharing.
These distinctions are important, but they may be difficult for ordinary users to understand. Many users may not know the difference between health data, usage data, technical data, device identifiers, advertising identifiers, app events, and inferences. A company may say that it does not share “health data” with advertisers, but still use technical identifiers or app events for advertising attribution. This may be legally different, but from the user’s perspective it can still feel privacy-relevant.
Femtech apps should therefore provide a clear processing separation table:










Processing Type
Required for App Function?
Data Used
Third Parties Involved
User Choice




Core service
Yes
Account data, reproductive health data
Hosting and essential service providers
Required to use app


Health insights and predictions
Usually yes
Menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, symptom data
Internal systems or limited providers
Required/optional depending on feature


App analytics
No/Partly
Usage data, device data, crash data
Analytics providers
Opt-out should be available


Advertising attribution
No
Device identifiers, app events, ad IDs
Advertising platforms and attribution providers
Opt-in should be required


Research
No
Deidentified or consented health data
Research partners
Separate consent should be required


Employer or health-plan benefits
No, except for Ovia+
Eligibility or benefit-related data
Employer, health plan, provider partners
Separate disclosure and consent required



This separation would make consent more meaningful because users could distinguish between data processing that is necessary and data processing that is optional or secondary. Users should not have to accept advertising, research, or broad analytics uses simply because they want basic period tracking or pregnancy support.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Separate Opt-In Consent for Secondary Uses of Reproductive Health Data
text:
Require Separate Opt-In Consent for Secondary Uses of Reproductive Health Data
The sixth recommendation is that femtech apps should obtain separate opt-in consent for secondary uses of reproductive health data. Secondary uses include advertising, marketing, research, product development beyond core service delivery, deidentified data sharing, aggregated data sharing, employer or health-plan reporting, and commercial partnerships.
This recommendation responds directly to the ethical issue identified in the project: reproductive health data is context-sensitive. Users may provide menstrual or pregnancy data for personal health tracking, but that does not mean they expect the data to be used for advertising optimization, research partnerships, commercial datasets, or employer benefit services.
Separate opt-in consent should be required for:

sharing reproductive health data with research partners;
using reproductive health data to create deidentified datasets;
sharing or selling deidentified or aggregated data;
using reproductive health data for product development beyond core app functionality;
using related inferences for recommendations or marketing;
connecting app data to employer, health plan, or health care provider programs;
using technical or behavioral data for advertising attribution.

This opt-in should be specific. Instead of one broad consent statement, users should be able to make separate choices. For example:








Secondary Use
Consent Required?
Default Setting




Use health data for cycle predictions
Yes, as part of core service
On if user uses feature


Share deidentified health data for academic research
Yes
Off by default


Share deidentified or aggregated data with commercial partners
Yes
Off by default


Use device identifiers for advertising attribution
Yes
Off by default


Send personalized product recommendations based on health data
Yes
Off by default


Connect data to employer or health-plan benefits
Yes
Off by default



This recommendation would help protect user autonomy. It would also reduce the problem of bundled consent, where users must accept many types of data processing in order to use a basic service.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Strengthen Rules for Deidentified and Aggregated Data
text:
Strengthen Rules for Deidentified and Aggregated Data
The seventh recommendation is that femtech apps should provide clearer disclosure and stronger consent rules for deidentified and aggregated data. This is especially important because reproductive health data can remain sensitive even when identifiers are removed.
Ovia is the clearest example of why this matters. Its policy states that it may use personal data to create deidentified data for research and development, provide deidentified data to research partners, provide data to other businesses that engage Ovia for research services, disclose or sell deidentified data derived from patient information, and receive compensation for sharing deidentified or aggregate data. This is disclosed, but it raises important consent questions. Users may not understand that data they enter for fertility, pregnancy, or parenting support could later contribute to deidentified datasets used for research, product development, or compensated sharing.
Deidentification can reduce privacy risk, but it should not eliminate transparency obligations. Users should be told:

what data may be deidentified;
how deidentification is performed;
whether deidentified data may be shared;
who may receive it;
whether it may be sold or used for compensation;
whether it may be combined with other datasets;
whether users can opt out.

A stronger disclosure could look like this:







Question
Required Disclosure




Will my reproductive health data be used to create deidentified data?
Yes/No


What types of data may be included?
Menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, symptoms, app usage, etc.


Who may receive deidentified data?
Researchers, universities, medical institutions, commercial partners, internal teams


Can the company receive compensation?
Yes/No


Can I opt out?
Yes/No


Will the company attempt to reidentify me?
No, with enforceable commitment



This recommendation is important because users may not understand the difference between personal data, deidentified data, and aggregated data. Companies should not rely on technical definitions alone. They should explain these concepts in plain language and provide meaningful choices.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Clearer Data Retention and Deletion Standards
text:
Require Clearer Data Retention and Deletion Standards
The eighth recommendation is that femtech apps should provide clear and specific data retention periods. Users should not have to guess how long their reproductive health data will remain stored.
The three apps vary in how they describe retention. Flo says it retains data as long as necessary and may retain inactive account data for three years. Clue repeatedly says data is deleted when it is no longer required for the purpose for which it was collected, and users can request deletion within one month. Ovia provides a specific standard for Consumer users: it stores all categories of data while the account is active and for seven years afterward.
Specific retention periods are easier for users to understand than broad phrases such as “as long as necessary.” A stronger policy standard should require apps to provide a retention table:









Data Category
Retention Period
What Happens After Deletion Request?
Backup Deletion Timeline




Account data
Specific period
Deleted or anonymized
Specific timeline


Reproductive health data
Specific period
Deleted or anonymized
Specific timeline


Device and usage data
Specific period
Deleted or anonymized
Specific timeline


Advertising identifiers
Specific period
Deleted or anonymized
Specific timeline


Research data
Specific period or study duration
Deleted, anonymized, or retained under consent
Specific timeline


Deidentified data
Specific period or indefinite
Explain whether deletion applies
Specific timeline



Data retention is central to informed consent because users may be comfortable sharing data temporarily but not indefinitely. For example, a user may use a pregnancy app during pregnancy but may not expect pregnancy-related data to remain stored for many years after the account becomes inactive. Femtech apps should therefore make retention periods visible and specific.
Users should also be able to delete their data inside the app without having to search for a support email. Data deletion should be available through an accessible account setting, and the app should confirm what data has been deleted, what data may remain, and why.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Special Protection for Employer, Health Plan, and Ovia+ Contexts
text:
Require Special Protection for Employer, Health Plan, and Ovia+ Contexts
The ninth recommendation is that apps connected to employers, health plans, or health care providers should follow stricter disclosure standards. This recommendation is especially relevant to Ovia because its policy distinguishes between Consumer users and Ovia+ users, where Ovia+ may be provided through employers, health plans, or health care providers.
Employer and health plan contexts raise special privacy concerns. Users may worry that reproductive health information could affect employment, insurance, benefits, or institutional treatment, even if the company states that sensitive data is not shared in that way. Because of this power imbalance, ordinary privacy policy disclosure is not enough.
For apps connected to employers or health plans, users should receive a separate disclosure answering:







Question
Required Answer




Is my employer involved?
Yes/No


Is my health plan involved?
Yes/No


What data does the employer or health plan receive?
Specific categories


Does the employer or health plan receive individual-level health data?
Yes/No


Does the employer or health plan receive aggregated reports?
Yes/No


Can my app use affect my benefits?
Yes/No


Can I use the app without employer or health-plan data sharing?
Yes/No



This recommendation is based on the idea that consent is affected by context and power. A user using a consumer period tracker is in a different position from a user accessing an app through an employer-sponsored health benefit. In employer or health-plan settings, users may feel pressure to participate or may not fully understand what information flows back to the institution. Therefore, stronger disclosure and opt-in consent are necessary.

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Require Auditable Privacy Claims

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Policy Implementation: A Femtech Privacy Disclosure Standard

title: Policy Recommendations
section: Summary
text:
Summary
The main policy goal should be to move from formal notice to meaningful informed consent. Flo, Clue, and Ovia all provide privacy disclosures, but the findings show that users still face a heavy interpretation burden. The problem is not simply that companies fail to disclose information. The problem is that disclosures are often too complex, broad, or scattered to support realistic user understanding.
The following table summarizes the recommendations:








Recommendation
Problem Addressed
Expected Benefit




Standardized plain-language privacy summary
Policies are too long and complex
Helps users quickly understand key data practices


Sensitive reproductive data labels
Sensitive data is sometimes placed in broad categories
Makes reproductive health data visible as a special category


Standardized data use table
Users must search across many sections
Makes collection, use, sharing, ads, analytics, and retention easier to compare


Specific third-party sharing disclosure
Terms like “partners” and “providers” are vague
Shows who receives data and why


Separation of core service, analytics, and advertising
Users may not understand different processing purposes
Clarifies what is necessary and what is optional


Separate opt-in for secondary uses
Consent is often bundled
Gives users more meaningful control


Stronger rules for deidentified and aggregated data
Users may not understand commercial or research uses
Improves transparency around data reuse


Clear retention and deletion standards
Retention language can be vague
Helps users know how long data remains stored


Special protection for employer and health-plan contexts
Institutional relationships create power concerns
Protects users in benefit-based app programs


Auditable privacy claims
Policies are self-reported
Makes privacy promises more trustworthy



Overall, these recommendations would make femtech privacy policies more specific, comparable, and user-centered. They would also better align privacy disclosure with the sensitivity of reproductive health data. Meaningful informed consent should not depend on whether a user can interpret a long legal document. It should depend on whether the company provides clear, structured, and actionable information about how sensitive data is collected, shared, used, retained, and protected.

title: Data and Methods
text:
This project uses publicly available privacy policy documents as its primary data source. The sample includes the privacy policies of three menstrual tracking and women’s health applications: Flo, Clue, and Ovia. These documents are treated as textual data because they contain the official language through which each company explains how it collects, uses, shares, analyzes, stores, and protects users’ personal and reproductive health information.
Unlike a quantitative dataset made up of rows and variables, the data in this project consists of policy texts. These texts are important because privacy policies are one of the main ways digital health companies communicate their data practices to users. In the notice-and-consent model of digital privacy, users are often expected to read or accept these policies before using a service. Therefore, the wording, structure, specificity, and readability of the privacy policy are central to whether user consent can be considered meaningful.
The three privacy policies analyzed in this project are:

Flo Privacy Policy: Flo is a menstrual cycle, ovulation, fertility, pregnancy, and women’s health tracking app. Its privacy policy describes the collection of account information, menstrual cycle details, pregnancy-related information, symptoms, sex life, physical and mental health information, device information, usage data, and data from third-party services such as Apple HealthKit and Google Health Connect. It also discusses advertising, analytics, service providers, commercial partners, data retention, and security.
Clue Privacy Policy: Clue is a period and reproductive health tracking app. Its privacy policy is organized around three major types of data: account data, usage data, and health data. Clue defines health data as information users choose to track in the app, including cycle information, period length, pain, spotting, weight, body temperature, hair quality, sexual intercourse, and data imported from integrations such as Apple Health or supported fitness trackers. The policy also discusses essential third-party providers, scientific research, health analytics, advertising optimization, website analytics, newsletters, surveys, data transfers, rights, and security procedures.
Ovia Health by Labcorp Apps Privacy Policy: Ovia provides fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, and related services. Its policy covers both the free consumer version of the app and the enhanced Ovia+ version, which may be provided through employers, health plans, or health care providers. Ovia’s policy lists broad categories of data, including identifiers, health information, legally protected classifications, biometric information, internet and app activity, geolocation, audio and visual information, professional and employment information, inferences, and sensitive information. It also discusses advertising, analytics, data retention, deidentified data, aggregated data, research, and employer or health plan contexts.




Flo, Clue, and Ovia were selected because they represent widely recognized examples of femtech and reproductive health applications. Each app is connected to menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, or reproductive health tracking, and each collects information that may be highly sensitive. These apps are especially relevant for a data ethics analysis because the information users enter into them may include menstrual cycle patterns, pregnancy status, fertility goals, symptoms, sexual activity, physical health, mental health, and other intimate details.
The three apps also provide useful variation for comparison. Flo’s policy is detailed and includes specific discussion of advertising and analytics partners, such as AppsFlyer, Firebase, and TikTok Ad Manager. Clue’s policy is more clearly structured around account data, usage data, and health data, and repeatedly emphasizes that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers. Ovia’s policy has the broadest scope because it covers fertility, pregnancy, parenting, health coaching, employer or health-plan sponsored services, deidentified data, aggregated data, and research-related uses. Comparing these three policies makes it possible to examine not only whether privacy disclosures exist, but also how clear, specific, and usable those disclosures are.
This sample is not intended to represent every femtech app on the market. Instead, the purpose is to conduct a focused comparative textual analysis of three prominent cases. The sample is appropriate for this project because the goal is not to make a statistical claim about all femtech companies, but to analyze how privacy policy language operates in a high-sensitivity data context.



The privacy policy texts were collected manually from the official websites of each company. Manual collection was appropriate for this project because the sample size is small and because the analysis requires close reading rather than large-scale automated scraping. The goal was to identify the sections of each policy that describe data collection, data use, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, security, and sensitive reproductive health information.
For each privacy policy, I reviewed the table of contents or section headings and focused on portions of the policy most relevant to informed consent. These included sections describing personal data collected from users, how personal data is used, whether data is shared with third parties, whether advertising or analytics partners receive data, how long data is retained, and whether sensitive reproductive health data is treated separately from general personal data.
During data collection, I recorded key phrases and policy language that directly described data practices. Examples include terms such as “health data,” “special category data,” “sensitive information,” “third-party providers,” “advertising,” “analytics,” “research partners,” “deidentified data,” “aggregated data,” “legitimate interests,” “may share,” and “as permitted by law.” These phrases became the basis for the coding categories used in the analysis.

text:

It also discusses advertising, analytics, data retention, deidentified data, aggregated data, research, and employer or health plan contexts.




Flo, Clue, and Ovia were selected because they represent widely recognized examples of femtech and reproductive health applications. Each app is connected to menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, or reproductive health tracking, and each collects information that may be highly sensitive. These apps are especially relevant for a data ethics analysis because the information users enter into them may include menstrual cycle patterns, pregnancy status, fertility goals, symptoms, sexual activity, physical health, mental health, and other intimate details.
The three apps also provide useful variation for comparison. Flo’s policy is detailed and includes specific discussion of advertising and analytics partners, such as AppsFlyer, Firebase, and TikTok Ad Manager. Clue’s policy is more clearly structured around account data, usage data, and health data, and repeatedly emphasizes that tracked health data is not shared with advertisers. This sample is not intended to represent every femtech app on the market. Instead, the purpose is to conduct a focused comparative textual analysis of three prominent cases. The sample is appropriate for this project because the goal is not to make a statistical claim about all femtech companies, but to analyze how privacy policy language operates in a high-sensitivity data context.



The privacy policy texts were collected manually from the official websites of each company. Manual collection was appropriate for this project because the sample size is small and because the analysis requires close reading rather than large-scale automated scraping. The goal was to identify the sections of each policy that describe data collection, data use, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, security, and sensitive reproductive health information.
For each privacy policy, I reviewed the table of contents or section headings and focused on portions of the policy most relevant to informed consent. These included sections describing personal data collected from users, how personal data is used, whether data is shared with third parties, whether advertising or analytics partners receive data, how long data is retained, and whether sensitive reproductive health data is treated separately from general personal data.
During data collection, I recorded key phrases and policy language that directly described data practices. Examples include terms such as “health data,” “special category data,” “sensitive information,” “third-party providers,” “advertising,” “analytics,” “research partners,” “deidentified data,” “aggregated data,” “legitimate interests,” “may share,” and “as permitted by law.” These phrases became the basis for the coding categories used in the analysis.

title: Data and Methods
section: Method
text:
Method

Analytical Method: Comparative Textual Analysis
This project uses comparative textual analysis as its main method. Comparative textual analysis is appropriate because the research question focuses on language, disclosure, and interpretability. Rather than measuring user behavior directly, this method examines how the companies themselves describe their data practices and whether those descriptions are specific enough to support meaningful informed consent.
The analysis proceeded in three stages. First, I conducted a close reading of each privacy policy. I identified the sections that discuss sensitive reproductive health data, data collection, data use, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, research, retention, and user rights. Second, I coded each policy according to a shared set of categories. Third, I compared the three apps to evaluate differences in clarity, specificity, and complexity.
The coding process was designed to connect the empirical privacy policy texts to the project’s central conceptual question: whether formal disclosure is sufficient for meaningful informed consent. A policy may technically disclose data practices, but if those disclosures are scattered, vague, overly broad, or difficult to understand, then users may still be unable to make an informed choice about whether to use the app or share sensitive data.


Coding Categories
Each privacy policy was coded using the same categories. These categories were selected because they reflect the main dimensions of informed consent in the context of reproductive health data. In particular, the coding scheme focuses on whether users can understand what data is collected, why it is collected, who receives it, whether it is used for advertising or analytics, how long it is retained, and whether the policy uses broad or ambiguous language.







Coding Category
Analytical Purpose




What sensitive data is collected?
Identifies whether the app collects menstrual, fertility, pregnancy, sexual activity, symptoms, physical health, mental health, or other reproductive health information.


How is data collection explained?
Examines whether the policy clearly distinguishes between data provided directly by users, data collected automatically, data imported from other services, and data received from external sources.


Is sensitive reproductive data clearly labeled?
Evaluates whether the policy explicitly names reproductive health data or places it inside broader categories such as “health information,” “personal data,” or “sensitive information.”


Is third-party sharing mentioned?
Identifies whether the policy explains sharing with service providers, advertising partners, analytics providers, research partners, commercial partners, employers, health plans, or other third parties.


Is advertising mentioned?
Examines whether the policy discloses the use of personal data for advertising, marketing, ad attribution, retargeting, or campaign optimization.


Is analytics mentioned?
Examines whether the policy discusses app analytics, health analytics, website analytics, research analytics, technical performance analytics, or advertising analytics.


What vague phrases appear?
Identifies broad or ambiguous language, such as “may share,” “legitimate interests,” “business purposes,” “partners,” “as permitted by law,” or “improve services.”


Does it have a plain-language summary?
Assesses whether the policy uses summaries, tables, examples, visual explanations, or clear section headings that make the policy easier for users to understand.


How long is data retained?
Examines whether the policy clearly explains retention periods, account deletion, backup deletion, inactivity rules, and exceptions to deletion.





Operationalizing Meaningful Informed Consent
A key methodological issue in this project is how to operationalize “meaningful informed consent.” In many digital privacy settings, informed consent is weakly operationalized as the existence of a privacy policy or the user’s formal agreement to the policy. However, this project treats that operationalization as insufficient. The mere presence of a privacy policy does not show that users can understand how their sensitive data is collected, shared, analyzed, retained, or used for secondary purposes.
In this project, the conceptual variable is meaningful informed consent. The weaker operationalization would be whether a privacy policy exists. A stronger operationalization asks whether the privacy policy is clear, specific, readable, and usable for ordinary users. Therefore, this project evaluates privacy policies not only by whether they disclose data practices, but also by whether those disclosures are understandable and actionable.







Concept
Operationalization in This Project




Conceptual variable
Meaningful informed consent


Weak operationalization
The mere existence of a privacy policy or a formal user agreement


Stronger operationalization
Whether the policy clearly explains sensitive data collection, data use, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, and user choices



This operationalization is especially important because reproductive health data is highly sensitive. Users may reasonably expect information about menstrual cycles, fertility goals, pregnancy, sexual activity, symptoms, and health conditions to be handled differently from ordinary app usage data. If a policy does not clearly explain how these categories are collected and used, then user consent may be formal rather than meaningful.


Limitations
This project has several limitations. First, it analyzes privacy policy language rather than actual company data practices. A privacy policy describes what a company says it does, but it does not independently verify what happens in the app’s technical infrastructure. Second, the project analyzes three apps rather than the entire femtech market. The findings therefore should be understood as a focused comparison rather than a general statistical claim. Third, the analysis does not include user interviews or surveys, so it cannot directly measure whether users actually understand these privacy policies.
Despite these limitations, privacy policies are still important objects of analysis. They are the primary documents through which companies present their data practices to users, regulators, and the public. If these documents are vague or difficult to understand, that itself is a meaningful data ethics problem. In a context involving sensitive reproductive health data, the quality of disclosure is directly connected to whether consent can be considered informed.


Summary of Methodological Approach
In summary, this project analyzes the privacy policies of Flo, Clue, and Ovia as textual data. It uses comparative textual analysis to examine how each app describes sensitive reproductive health data, third-party sharing, advertising, analytics, retention, and user choices. The analysis is guided by the course concept of operationalization: rather than assuming that the existence of a privacy policy equals informed consent, this project evaluates whether each policy provides clear, specific, and usable disclosure. This approach allows the project to compare formal privacy disclosure with the stronger ethical standard of meaningful informed consent.
